nativeRange.habitat: Forested peat swamp streams and slow-moving blackwater; typically heavily tannin-stained water with low mineral content and leaf litter substrate

decorPreferences: Dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Cryptocoryne, Anubias, Microsorium)
Driftwood and leaf litter for tannins

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flake and micropellet foods; benefits from regular supplementation with small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, and bloodworm

avoidWith: Very boisterous or fast-feeding species that may outcompete them

summary: Iconic SE Asian schooling fish with a distinctive black triangle marking; peaceful, hardy, and generally considered an excellent choice for planted community aquariums.
careNotes: Harlequin Rasboras tend to show their best colors and most natural behavior when kept in schools of 8 or more in a well-planted aquarium with soft, slightly acidic water. They are generally very tolerant of a wide parameter range, but have been reported to thrive most notably in softer, more acidic conditions that reflect their blackwater peat swamp origins (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5 preferred). A dark substrate and subdued or diffused lighting appear to reduce stress and encourage more confident schooling. Driftwood and Indian almond leaves can help lower pH and add beneficial tannins. Mid-level swimmers that tend to congregate in the open water column. They are sometimes sold under the trade names 'Rasbora Het' or 'Red Rasbora'. Taxonomy note: FishBase places this species in family Danionidae (a subfamily of Cyprinidae elevated to family status in some classifications); older literature commonly lists it under Cyprinidae.
breedingNotes: Generally considered moderately challenging to breed in captivity. Spawning tends to occur most successfully in soft, acidic conditioning water (pH 5.3–6.0, gH 1–3, temperature 24–27°C). A distinguishing and unusual trait is that eggs are deposited on the underside of broad-leaved plants — Cryptocoryne species are commonly used, with the fish swimming inverted briefly to adhere eggs to the leaf surface. Typically 80–100 eggs per spawning; eggs have been reported to hatch within 24–48 hours and fry become free-swimming within roughly a week. Parents should be removed after spawning as they may consume eggs and fry. Older, well-conditioned adults (1+ years) tend to breed more successfully than juveniles.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for all attempted species-page URLs; SeriouslyFish is the first listed secondary for fish-freshwater slice and was used as primary. Most comprehensive source for water parameters, tank sizing, and breeding behavior.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (1.75–2 in = 4.4–5.1 cm, consistent with SeriouslyFish 3.5–4.5 cm), temperature (22–27°C consistent), pH (6.0–7.8 — slightly wider upper bound). Tank min of 10 gal (38L) cited for 6 fish; 20–30 gal preferred. Lifespan 5–8 years sourced here.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Danionidae), order (Cypriniformes), native range (Malay Peninsula, Singapore, Indonesia). Max size 5.0 cm. Temperature 22–25°C, pH 5.0–7.0, hardness 5–12 dH — consistent with other sources on lower-end parameters.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Article covering T. heteromorpha and T. espei together. Confirmed omnivore, schooling, beginner-level care, 10-gal minimum for 6 fish, mid-to-upper swim zone, need for live plants. Temperature ~76°F (~24°C), pH ~7.2 cited — pH higher than other sources, likely reflecting hobbyist tap-water keeping rather than ideal conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: Unusual inverted egg-adhesion behavior: eggs deposited on the underside of broad plant leaves (Cryptocoryne preferred). Requires soft acidic conditioning water. Parents eat eggs and fry; remove after spawning.
